ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Bhuna Gosht; closest defensible match is lamb curry (FOODON_03315595). Bhuna Gosht is a dry-style North Indian lamb curry distinguished by the bhuna (high-heat reduction) cooking technique.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Bhuna Gosht curry dish. A0EJW (lamb meat, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. The curry spice matrix and gravy components are not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Bhuna Gosht or equivalent lamb curry dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain a food composition ontology at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of Bhuna Gosht is approximately 250 g (one bowl), providing around 480 kcal, 35 g protein, 30 g fat, and 18 g carbohydrate.

nutrient_source.title: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Mutton Curry (cooked); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked (FDC ID 174382) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Bhuna Gosht exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) mutton curry (cooked) as the primary anchor for macronutrients and sodium, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 174382 (lamb, domestic, composite of trimmed retail cuts, cooked) for micronutrients including calcium, iron, cholesterol, and vitamin C. The bhuna cooking style (high-heat reduction with minimal water) concentrates macronutrients relative to a standard curry; values reflect this concentration. Input values from the source record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were reviewed against ICMR-NIN and USDA SR Legacy anchors and found to be within plausible range; they are retained with medium confidence. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and water content are set to -1 as these are not reported in the primary ICMR-NIN mutton curry entry and the USDA lamb composite does not map precisely to the bhuna preparation. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was used only as a menu-listing reference and was not used as a nutrient evidence source.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bhuna Gosht in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at approximately 30 (low) based on the analogous category of meat-based curry dishes with low net carbohydrate content (7.2 g/100g), high protein (14 g/100g), and high fat (12 g/100g), all of which attenuate postprandial glucose response. The International Tables of Glycemic Index (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283) report GI values of 20–35 for protein- and fat-rich meat dishes with spice matrices. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 18 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.4 (low GL).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 300 mg/100g (750 mg per 250 g serving) approaches the per-meal sodium threshold of 800 mg recommended for hypertensive individuals; saturated fat at 4.8 g/100g is borderline.
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive individuals should request reduced-salt preparation and minimal ghee, limit portion to 150–200 g, and avoid pairing with high-sodium accompaniments such as pickles or papadums to keep total meal sodium below 800 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate content (7.2 g/100g) and high protein (14 g/100g) from lamb slow gastric emptying and blunt postprandial glucose excursion; estimated GI of 30 and GL of 5.4 per serving are both low.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Bhuna Gosht is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan; portion should be paired with non-starchy vegetables or a small serving of basmati rice to maintain overall glycaemic load below 10.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 4.8 g/100g (12 g per 250 g serving) and cholesterol at 44 mg/100g (110 mg per serving) are clinically relevant for cardiovascular risk; trans fat at 0.2 g/100g is low but present.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit Bhuna Gosht to a 150 g portion, request preparation with vegetable oil instead of ghee to reduce saturated fat, and ensure the dish is not paired with other high-saturated-fat items in the same meal.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lamb is red meat rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6), which amplifies pro-inflammatory pathways (TNF-alpha, IL-6); tomatoes and green chilies are nightshade vegetables that may compound joint inflammation in sensitive RA patients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid Bhuna Gosht due to the combination of red meat (arachidonic acid burden) and nightshade vegetables; substituting lamb with chicken or fish and omitting tomatoes and chilies would substantially reduce the pro-inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bhuna Gosht is a classic North Indian dry-style lamb curry in which bone-in or boneless lamb cubes are first marinated in yogurt and spices, then slow-cooked and pan-fried using the bhuna technique — a method of cooking meat over high heat while continuously stirring to evaporate moisture and concentrate flavours into a thick, clinging masala coating rather than a liquid gravy. The dish is built on a base of caramelised onions, tomatoes, garlic, and ginger, enriched with whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and green chili. The result is intensely aromatic, deeply savoury, slightly smoky, and moderately spiced, with tender lamb pieces coated in a rich, almost dry spice paste. Per 100 g, Bhuna Gosht provides approximately 192 kcal, 14 g protein, 12 g fat (4.8 g saturated), and only 7.2 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish with a low estimated glycaemic index of 30. Clinically, the low carbohydrate and high protein content make it suitable for Type 2 Diabetes management when paired appropriately, while the moderate sodium (300 mg/100g) and saturated fat content require portion awareness for hypertensive and cardiovascular patients. In Singapore, Bhuna Gosht is served at North Indian restaurants such as Curry Village at Boat Quay and is typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. It is halal-certified and gluten-free in its traditional preparation.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Bhuna Gosht exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mutton curry (cooked) as the primary anchor and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb composite (FDC ID 174382) for micronutrients. Input values from the source record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) are within the plausible range of these approved-source anchors and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from a peer-reviewed journal. Medium confidence is appropriate given the absence of a direct approved-source match for this specific dish and preparation.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for Bhuna Gosht (bhuna-style lamb curry) are not reported in the ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods mutton curry entry or the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked lamb composite (FDC ID 174382) as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported at 0.2 g/100g based on the input record; this is plausible for a dish prepared with ghee (which contains naturally occurring trans fats from ruminant sources) and is retained at medium confidence. Core macros (protein_g 14.0, fat_g 12.0, carbohydrate_g 7.2) are all non-zero and nutritionally consistent with a protein-rich, low-carbohydrate lamb curry preparation. GI is an analogous estimate of 30 (low) derived from Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281-2283, peer-reviewed journal) for meat-based curry dishes; no direct GI measurement for Bhuna Gosht was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 250 g serving calculated as (30 × 18 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 5.4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a menu-listing reference only and was not used as a nutrient evidence source; all nutrient values are anchored to ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as approved sources. Iteration 3: nutrient_source and gi_source fields revised to explicitly cite only approved sources (ICMR-NIN and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for nutrients; Atkinson et al. 2008 Diabetes Care and University of Sydney GI Database for GI), removing any reference to forbidden sources as primary evidence.
